evidence: Sessions 2134216b and 7eb84ce4 show multiple bitsmith invocations (5 and 3 respectively) with zero ruinor review invocations. The orchestrator config explicitly states 'Do not skip Ruinor review. All plans and implementations must be reviewed by Ruinor (mandatory baseline).' yet these sessions completed without any review gate.
inference: The orchestrator (dungeonmaster) is treating some tasks as 'trivial' and skipping the mandatory review gate entirely. The config does allow skipping Pathfinder for trivial tasks but does not explicitly exempt trivial tasks from ruinor review. The wording 'Optional Implementation Review: For trivial changes, may skip review entirely OR run Ruinor only' in Example 2 of the orchestrator config contradicts the mandatory constraint stated earlier, creating ambiguity.

proposed_change.change_description: Remove the contradictory language in Example 2 that says 'For trivial changes, may skip review entirely OR run Ruinor only'. Replace with: 'For trivial changes, run Ruinor only (mandatory baseline still applies). Skip specialist reviewers.' This resolves the contradiction between the mandatory constraint and the trivial-task exemption.

expected_benefit: Ruinor review should be invoked in all sessions, including those with trivial single-step tasks, eliminating sessions where implementation ships with zero quality gate.
tradeoffs: May add latency to truly trivial changes (variable rename, typo fix) where review adds no value. Could increase ruinor invocation count by 20-30%.
evaluation_plan: After applying the wording change, observe the next 5 sessions that involve trivial tasks. Success: ruinor is invoked in all of them. Failure: sessions still skip ruinor, indicating the orchestrator is ignoring the constraint for another reason.

evidence: Sessions 2134216b, 7eb84ce4, and bd8247d8 show invocations of agent types 'general-purpose' and 'claude-code-guide' which are not defined in the agent team configuration. Session 2134216b has 2 claude-code-guide and 1 general-purpose invocation. Session 7eb84ce4 has 1 claude-code-guide and 1 general-purpose. Session bd8247d8 has 1 general-purpose.
inference: The orchestrator or the underlying system is routing some tasks to default/fallback agent types instead of the defined team agents (bitsmith, pathfinder, etc.). This may occur when the orchestrator does not recognize a task as fitting any defined agent role, or when the system falls back to a generic agent type for informational queries. These invocations represent work happening outside the defined coordination structure with no quality oversight.

proposed_change.change_description: Add a constraint to the orchestrator: 'Do not delegate to generic or unnamed agent types. All delegation must go to named team agents: Pathfinder (planning), Bitsmith (implementation), Ruinor (review), Riskmancer (security), Windwarden (performance), Knotcutter (complexity), Quill (documentation), Everwise (meta-analysis). If a task does not fit any agent, handle it directly or clarify with the user.'

expected_benefit: Elimination of untracked work being routed to generic agents outside the team structure. All work would flow through agents with defined quality standards and review protocols.
tradeoffs: Some informational or exploratory queries that currently go to generic agents may need to be handled by the orchestrator directly, increasing its workload. There may be legitimate uses of generic agents for quick lookups that would become more cumbersome.
evaluation_plan: After applying the routing rule, observe the next 5 sessions. Success: zero invocations of 'general-purpose' or 'claude-code-guide' agent types. Failure: these agent types still appear, indicating the routing happens at a system level below the orchestrator config.

evidence: Session 2134216b shows 5 consecutive bitsmith invocations with no review gate between any of them. Session 7eb84ce4 shows 3 consecutive bitsmith invocations with no intermediate review. The orchestrator protocol specifies that after implementation, a mandatory review gate should run (Phase 4: Implementation Review).
inference: The orchestrator appears to be batching multiple implementation tasks into a single execution phase and only running the review gate once at the end (or not at all, per EVW-2026-03-23-001). This means multiple code changes accumulate without quality verification between them, increasing the blast radius of any defect and making review harder when it does occur.

proposed_change.change_description: Add clarification to Phase 3 (Execution): 'When multiple implementation tasks are delegated to Bitsmith, run Ruinor review after each logically complete unit of work, not only after all tasks are completed. A logically complete unit is one that could be independently reviewed and verified.'

expected_benefit: Earlier defect detection, smaller review scope per gate, reduced rework when issues are found late in a batch of changes.
tradeoffs: Increases the number of ruinor invocations per session, adding latency and cost. For truly independent trivial changes, per-change review may be excessive.
evaluation_plan: After applying the change, observe the next 5 sessions with multiple bitsmith invocations. Success: ruinor is invoked between logically distinct implementation units. Failure: batched bitsmith invocations still proceed without intermediate review.

evidence: Session cf95f68b shows everwise invoked twice within the session (at 03:29:59Z and 03:53:08Z). The second invocation occurs immediately after a ruinor REVISE verdict on bitsmith output (ruinor REVISE at 03:51:28Z recorded without a verdict string — the ruinor entry at line 9 has verdict null, then everwise fires at 03:53:08Z before the next bitsmith at 03:56:57Z). Everwise's own config explicitly states she is 'invoked manually by the user when they want a meta-analysis of recent sessions' and 'does not invoke other agents' and 'does not escalate or block work.' The mid-session invocation pattern is inconsistent with her defined role.
inference: Either the orchestrator or the user is treating everwise as an escalation path or real-time reviewer when a ruinor REVISE verdict stalls progress. This is a persona misunderstanding: everwise is a post-session meta-analyst, not an in-session escalation agent. Her output is advisory, and she produces structured lessons, not operational fixes. Invoking her mid-session wastes a turn and produces no actionable output for the current session.

proposed_change.change_description: In the Important constraints section, add: 'Do not invoke Everwise to resolve in-session review failures or escalation stalls. Everwise is a post-session meta-analysis tool and produces advisory lessons, not operational decisions. If a review loop is stuck (3+ REVISE cycles on the same artifact), escalate to Pathfinder for plan revision, not to Everwise.'

expected_benefit: Everwise invocations are reserved for user-initiated post-session analysis. Mid-session Everwise invocations that produce no operational output are eliminated.
tradeoffs: If the mid-session everwise invocation was user-initiated rather than orchestrator-initiated, this constraint change will have no effect and the source of confusion is elsewhere.
evaluation_plan: Observe the next 10 sessions for mid-session everwise invocations (everwise appearing between other agent activations within the same session). Success: everwise invocations only appear at the start or end of a session, never sandwiched between operational agent steps. Failure: mid-session everwise invocations continue, suggesting user is initiating them and a different intervention is needed.

evidence: Session 45d4a4b8 is the only session across all 22 chronicles to produce a REJECT verdict from ruinor (at 16:36:18Z, on bitsmith output). After the REJECT, bitsmith was re-invoked (16:36:47Z) and produced output that ruinor then ACCEPTed (16:37:46Z) — a gap of only 29 seconds between ruinor REJECT and ruinor ACCEPT. A genuine REJECT should require substantial rework, yet the re-review passed in under 90 seconds total including the bitsmith fix time. This suggests either the REJECT was borderline (close to REVISE), or the fix was trivially small, or the re-review was not thorough.
inference: The REJECT verdict, defined as the most severe outcome requiring major rework, may be triggering on issues that a REVISE would also cover, creating a label-severity mismatch. Alternatively, the orchestrator is not enforcing a meaningful distinction between REJECT and REVISE in terms of response depth — both result in a single bitsmith pass followed by immediate re-review.

proposed_change.change_description: Add a post-REJECT gate to the orchestrator's review loop: when ruinor issues REJECT, the subsequent bitsmith fix must be accompanied by a written summary of what changed and why, passed as explicit context to the re-review invocation. This forces the fix to be documented and the re-review to be targeted, reducing rubber-stamp re-approvals after REJECT.

expected_benefit: REJECT verdicts produce substantive rework and targeted re-reviews rather than reflexive re-invocations that pass immediately. The time between REJECT and subsequent ACCEPT should increase, reflecting genuine remediation.
tradeoffs: Adds a documentation step to the remediation loop. May slow sessions where the fix is genuinely trivial (edge case in the rubric that happened to trigger REJECT threshold).
evaluation_plan: Monitor subsequent sessions for REJECT verdicts. For each REJECT, observe: (1) time elapsed between REJECT and next ACCEPT, (2) number of bitsmith invocations before ACCEPT. A sub-120-second REJECT-to-ACCEPT cycle with a single bitsmith call is a signal the remediation lacked depth. Observe at least 5 REJECT occurrences before drawing conclusions. Given REJECT appears in only 1 of 22 sessions, this may take many session batches to evaluate.
